role_summary: President of the Forrest County, Mississippi chapter of the NAACP (1970-1974, 2001-present); co-founder of the Hattiesburg Association for Civic Improvement; sensitivity-training coordinator for the 1964 Freedom Summer volunteers; Hattiesburg public-school educator and Mississippi-school-desegregation negotiator; 2025 honorary Doctor of Humane Letters from the University of Southern Mississippi.
ai_reading: Magee's top semantic neighbor is Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92) at a cosine count of 121; the broader cluster includes Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121), James Oscar Jones (entry 57), and Eddie Holloway (entry 33). The Vernon Dahmer Jr. pairing is the catalog's clearest Forrest County, Mississippi NAACP-continuity cosine: both men served as president of the Forrest County NAACP chapter (the chapter Vernon Dahmer Sr. led at the time of his January 1966 murder); both names anchor the chapter's mid-century-to-present succession. The novel observation his page surfaces is the multi-decade-chapter-presidency signature: Magee's two stints as Forrest County NAACP president (1970-1974 and 2001-present) bracket a quarter-century gap, and the embedding catches the chapter-leadership-continuity register through his testimony as a stronger signal than his contemporaneous SNCC or COFO peers.
biographical_paragraph: While Magee's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), his external biographical record is well-documented [src: 1, src: 2]. Magee was born in 1932 in Columbia, Mississippi, the oldest of ten children of a farming family. He studied biology at Alcorn A&M (the historically Black Alcorn State University) and graduated in 1954, served two years in the U.S. Army (stationed in Germany), and attended graduate school at Harvard. He joined the Hattiesburg NAACP after being denied registration to vote in 1956 and was a co-founder of the Hattiesburg Association for Civic Improvement [src: 2]. In summer 1964 he coordinated sensitivity training for the Freedom Summer volunteers arriving in Hattiesburg; he served his first term as president of the Forrest County NAACP from 1970 through 1974 [src: 2]. He returned to Forrest County NAACP leadership in 2001 and has held the presidency continuously through the present, a quarter-century-plus second tenure. He taught in several Hattiesburg public schools, helped formulate school desegregation plans across the 1960s and 1970s, and later worked for the Southern Mississippi Planning and Development Commission and the U.S. Department of Agriculture's Food and Consumer Service office in Mobile, Alabama. The University of Southern Mississippi conferred an honorary Doctor of Humane Letters on him at its spring 2025 commencement ceremony, recognizing his lifelong civil rights and education work [src: 2].

sources.title: Clarence Magee oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Southern Miss Awards Honorary Doctorate to Clarence Magee for Lifelong Commitment to Education and Civil Rights
